class_schedule.topic: HTML Foundations: Semantic Markup for Designers
learning_objectives: Write semantic HTML that communicates document structure to browsers and assistive technology
Understand the relationship between HTML structure and visual design
Build a well-structured web page from a design mockup using semantic elements
readings: HTML & CSS: Design and Build Websites by Jon Duckett, Ch. 1-4

lecture_notes.introduction: HTML is the structural foundation of every web interface. Understanding HTML lets UX designers communicate precisely with developers, build their own prototypes, and ensure their designs are implementable and accessible.
key_concepts: Semantic HTML: header, nav, main, section, article, aside, footer
Document structure: headings hierarchy (h1-h6), paragraphs, lists
Forms: input types, labels, fieldsets, and their UX implications
Links vs. buttons: when to use each and why it matters for accessibility
HTML as the accessibility layer: proper markup enables screen reader navigation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Converting a Figma design mockup into semantic HTML — discussing element choices and their accessibility implications at each step
setup_requirements: VS Code with Live Server extension

review.value: Using <nav> instead of <div class='nav'> communicates meaning to browsers and assistive technology. Semantic HTML is the foundation of accessibility — screen readers navigate by landmarks (nav, main, footer), not div classes.

Links (<a>) navigate to a new page or section. Buttons (<button>) perform an action on the current page. Using the wrong element breaks keyboard behavior and screen reader expectations — a critical accessibility distinction.

class_schedule.topic: CSS Layout: Flexbox and Grid for Designers
learning_objectives: Build responsive layouts using CSS Flexbox and Grid
Translate Figma auto-layout designs into CSS Flexbox code
Implement responsive behavior using media queries and container queries
readings: CSS-Tricks: A Complete Guide to Flexbox
CSS-Tricks: A Complete Guide to Grid

lecture_notes.introduction: CSS Flexbox and Grid are the layout tools that implement your Figma designs in the browser. Understanding them lets you design layouts that are naturally responsive and avoids proposing layouts that are impossible or expensive to implement.
key_concepts: Flexbox: one-dimensional layout (row OR column) — maps directly to Figma auto-layout
Grid: two-dimensional layout (rows AND columns) — for complex page structures
Responsive design: media queries for breakpoint-based layout changes
CSS custom properties (variables): the CSS equivalent of Figma design tokens
Box model: margin, border, padding, content — how spacing works in CSS
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a responsive card grid layout with CSS Grid that adapts from 1 column (mobile) to 2 columns (tablet) to 3 columns (desktop) using a single media query
setup_requirements: CSS Flexbox and Grid cheat sheets

review.value: Figma's auto-layout is a visual implementation of CSS Flexbox. If you can build it in Figma auto-layout, a developer can implement it with Flexbox. Understanding this connection improves designer-developer collaboration.

Grid handles the overall page structure (header, sidebar, main, footer) while Flexbox handles component-level layout (card contents, navigation items). Use both together for complete responsive pages.

class_schedule.topic: CSS Styling: Typography, Color, and Spacing
learning_objectives: Implement typography systems from design tokens to CSS
Apply color palettes and spacing scales using CSS custom properties
Build a basic CSS design token system that maps to Figma variables
readings: Refactoring UI by Adam Wathan & Steve Schoger, Ch. on CSS implementation
MDN Web Docs: CSS Custom Properties

lecture_notes.introduction: CSS is where design tokens become real. Understanding how your typography scale, color palette, and spacing system translate to CSS custom properties bridges the gap between design files and live code.
key_concepts: CSS custom properties: --color-primary, --spacing-md, --font-size-body
Typography in CSS: font-family, font-size, line-height, letter-spacing, font-weight
Color in CSS: hex, rgb, hsl — and why HSL is best for programmatic color manipulation
Spacing: margin and padding using custom properties from the design token scale
The cascade: how CSS specificity and inheritance affect your design implementation
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing a complete design token system in CSS custom properties, then building a styled card component using only token values — no hardcoded colors, sizes, or spacing

review.value: Figma variable 'color-primary: #2563EB' becomes CSS '--color-primary: #2563EB;'. Every design decision stored as a token has a direct CSS equivalent — this 1:1 mapping is why design systems work across design and code.
CSS applies styles based on specificity, inheritance, and source order. Understanding this prevents the 'why is my style not applying?' frustration that derails designer-developer collaboration.

class_schedule.topic: Interactive CSS: Transitions, Animations, and States
learning_objectives: Implement hover, focus, and active states using CSS pseudo-classes
Create smooth transitions and keyframe animations in CSS
Build accessible interactive states including focus-visible indicators
readings: Animation at Work by Rachel Nabors, Ch. 5-6
MDN Web Docs: CSS Transitions and Animations

lecture_notes.introduction: Interactive states and animations bring your interaction design specifications to life in the browser. Understanding CSS transitions and animations lets you prototype realistic interactions and specify them precisely for developers.
key_concepts: CSS pseudo-classes: :hover, :focus, :focus-visible, :active, :disabled
CSS transitions: property, duration, timing-function, delay
CSS keyframe animations: @keyframes for multi-step animations
Performance: transform and opacity are GPU-accelerated (smooth), most other properties are not
Reduced motion: @media (prefers-reduced-motion) for accessibility
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an interactive button component with all states (default, hover, focus, active, disabled, loading) using CSS transitions and a keyframe loading animation
setup_requirements: Browser developer tools with animation panel

review.value: Only transform (translate, scale, rotate) and opacity are GPU-accelerated in CSS. Animating these properties is smooth at 60fps. Animating width, height, margin, or color causes layout recalculation and can stutter — always prefer transform for movement.

:focus-visible shows focus styles only for keyboard navigation (not mouse clicks). This solves the 'ugly focus ring on click' problem while maintaining keyboard accessibility — use this instead of removing :focus styles.

learning_objectives: Use JavaScript to add interactivity to HTML/CSS prototypes
Handle user events: clicks, form inputs, scroll, and keyboard
Manipulate the DOM to show, hide, and update content dynamically
readings: Eloquent JavaScript by Marijn Haverbeke, Ch. 13-15

lecture_notes.introduction: JavaScript adds dynamic behavior to web pages. For UX designers, basic JavaScript skills enable building interactive prototypes that use real data and real interactions — far beyond what Figma prototyping can achieve.
key_concepts: DOM manipulation: selecting elements, changing content, toggling classes
Event handling: addEventListener for click, input, change, keydown, scroll
Conditional logic: showing and hiding elements based on user actions
Form handling: reading input values, validation, and dynamic form behavior
Fetch API basics: loading data from a JSON file or API for dynamic prototypes
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building an interactive search-with-filter prototype: typing in a search box filters a list of results in real time, clicking a result shows detail, and a clear button resets the view

review.value: JavaScript responds to user actions through event listeners: element.addEventListener('click', function). Every button click, form input, and scroll that triggers a change in the UI needs an event listener — this is how web interactivity works.

Changing what the user sees: document.querySelector('.message').textContent = 'Success!'. Toggle visibility: element.classList.toggle('hidden'). This is how dynamic interfaces work under the hood — understanding it helps you design implementable interactions.

learning_objectives: Combine HTML, CSS, and JavaScript into a fully interactive web prototype
Implement realistic form interactions with validation and feedback
Deploy a web prototype for remote user testing
readings: Sprint by Jake Knapp, Ch. on Realistic Prototypes

lecture_notes.introduction: Combining HTML, CSS, and JavaScript lets you build prototypes that look and behave like real products. These prototypes can be deployed to a URL and tested remotely — no special software required for testers.
key_concepts: Multi-page prototyping: linking HTML pages for navigation flow
Form prototyping: realistic inputs with validation feedback
Local storage: persisting user data between pages for multi-step flows
Deployment: publishing to Netlify or GitHub Pages for a shareable URL
Prototype vs. production code: prototype code prioritizes speed over maintainability
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a complete 3-page prototype with navigation, a form with validation, and data persistence between pages — then deploying it to Netlify for a shareable test URL
setup_requirements: Completed prototype components from previous sessions

review.value: localStorage.setItem('userName', 'Alex') persists data between pages in the same browser. This lets a prototype remember form inputs across a multi-step flow without any server — essential for realistic prototype testing.
Deploying to Netlify gives you a URL like myprototype.netlify.app that anyone can access on any device — no Figma account needed, no app installation. Ideal for remote unmoderated usability testing.

questions.question: A designer hands you a Figma mockup with a navigation bar containing 5 items spaced evenly across the top of the page. Which CSS layout method would you use to implement this, and why? Write the key CSS properties.
questions.answer: Use CSS Flexbox because this is a one-dimensional layout (items in a row). Key CSS: nav { display: flex; justify-content: space-between; align-items: center; padding: 0 var(--spacing-lg); }. This maps directly to Figma's auto-layout with 'Space Between' distribution. Flexbox is preferred over Grid here because the layout is single-axis (horizontal row). Space-between distributes items evenly with the first and last items at the edges. Align-items: center vertically centers items if they have different heights.

rubric.key: Correctly identifies Flexbox as the right approach
Explains why Flexbox over Grid for this case

questions.question: Why should a UX designer use CSS custom properties (variables) for colors and spacing instead of hardcoding hex values? Give a practical example of the benefit.
questions.answer: CSS custom properties provide a single source of truth for design decisions. Instead of hardcoding #2563EB in 47 places, you define --color-primary: #2563EB once and reference it everywhere with color: var(--color-primary). Practical benefit: when the brand color changes from blue to purple, you change ONE line of CSS and the entire site updates. Without custom properties, you search and replace across all files and risk missing instances or breaking unrelated styles. Additionally, custom properties enable dark mode by redefining token values: [data-theme='dark'] { --color-primary: #60A5FA; }. This maps directly to how design token systems work in Figma — 1:1 correspondence between design and code.

rubric.key: Explains single source of truth benefit
Provides practical example of the benefit
Mentions design token mapping or theme support

questions.question: Explain the difference between CSS :focus and :focus-visible pseudo-classes. Why should UX designers care about this distinction?
questions.answer: :focus applies styles whenever an element receives focus — from both keyboard (Tab) and mouse click. :focus-visible applies styles only when the browser determines the user is navigating via keyboard. UX designers should care because: (1) :focus on buttons shows a focus ring when clicked with a mouse, which many designers find visually undesirable. (2) Removing :focus to eliminate mouse click rings also removes keyboard focus indicators, breaking accessibility. (3) :focus-visible solves both problems: keyboard users see the focus ring (accessibility maintained), mouse users do not (visual design preserved). Best practice: style :focus-visible instead of :focus for interactive elements, and never use outline: none without a replacement.

rubric.key: Explains the practical UX problem this solves

questions.question: A UX designer wants to prototype a feature where clicking a 'Show More' button reveals hidden content on the page. Write the JavaScript pseudocode and explain which DOM manipulation methods you would use.
questions.answer: JavaScript pseudocode: (1) Select the button: const showMoreBtn = document.querySelector('.show-more-btn'). (2) Select the hidden content: const hiddenContent = document.querySelector('.extra-content'). (3) Add click event listener: showMoreBtn.addEventListener('click', function() { hiddenContent.classList.toggle('hidden'); if content is now visible, change button text to 'Show Less', else change to 'Show More'. }). DOM methods used: querySelector to select elements by CSS selector, addEventListener to respond to clicks, classList.toggle to add/remove a CSS class that controls visibility, and textContent to update the button label. The .hidden class in CSS would be: .hidden { display: none; }. This pattern — toggle a CSS class with JavaScript — is the fundamental interaction pattern for most show/hide behavior on the web.

rubric.key: Uses classList.toggle for CSS class manipulation

major_assignments.title: Code-Based Interactive Prototype
major_assignments.description: Build a fully interactive web prototype using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. The prototype must implement a multi-page user flow with realistic form interactions, validation, dynamic content, and be deployed to a shareable URL for user testing.
objectives: Build a multi-page web prototype from a Figma design
Implement interactive form behavior with validation and feedback
Use CSS custom properties as a design token system
Deploy the prototype and conduct a basic usability test
deliverables: CSS design token system mapping to Figma design tokens
Interactive form with real-time validation and error states
Deployed prototype on Netlify or GitHub Pages with shareable URL
Brief usability test report from testing the prototype with 2 users

criteria: Semantic HTML used correctly throughout
Document structure supports screen reader navigation
Forms use proper labels, fieldsets, and input types
Links and buttons used correctly for their purpose
CSS custom properties used for all design tokens
Responsive layout using Flexbox and/or Grid
Interactive states (hover, focus-visible, active, disabled) implemented
Transitions and animations are smooth and purposeful
Form validation provides real-time feedback
DOM manipulation creates dynamic content updates
Event handling is correct and efficient
Multi-page state persists using localStorage
Prototype deployed to a working URL
All pages and interactions function in the deployed version
Basic usability test conducted with 2 users
Findings documented with at least 2 actionable observations
Code is readable and reasonably organized
CSS follows a consistent naming convention
JavaScript is functional and handles edge cases
No console errors in the deployed version
Well-structured semantic HTML supporting accessibility
CSS design token system with responsive layout and interactive states
Functional JavaScript interactivity with form validation
Deployed and tested prototype with documented findings

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on HTML, CSS, and JavaScript fundamentals for UX prototyping

coding_labs.description: Weekly hands-on coding exercises building specific front-end skills

interactive_prototype.description: Complete code-based interactive prototype deployed and tested

code_reviews.description: Peer code reviews evaluating structure, readability, and accessibility

final_presentation.description: Presentation of prototype demonstrating how code skills enhance UX practice

course_policies.attendance: Lab sessions involve live coding instruction. Missing labs means missing foundational skills that compound. More than 3 absences affect grades.
course_policies.late_submissions: Coding labs build sequentially. Late submissions penalized 10% per day and may block subsequent exercises.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion and debugging help encouraged. All code must be written individually. Copy-pasting from classmates or tutorials without understanding is prohibited.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be your own or properly attributed. Using AI code generation tools is permitted for learning but all generated code must be understood and modified — submitting unmodified AI-generated code is prohibited.
course_policies.technology_requirements: VS Code with Live Server extension, modern web browser with developer tools, Netlify or GitHub account for deployment, and Figma for design reference.
